Just a great book that I read and it's by this guy named Mike, he's never pronounced his last name, some German guy I think, but anyway, more of this story is a micro business book and it's called Crop It First. Crop It First completely changed my life, more importantly, completely changed my business for the better. It showed me that in business, most of us are conditioned to think the problem. For conditional ratings, I don't know who, the thing that it's sales minus expenses equals profit, and that's very wrong, because what that leaves us with is a perspective that we have more money than we actually have, and we always forget that profit is a necessity in business. We always forget that profit is something that is a habit, it's not an event, meaning profit doesn't just happen in your business. Most of us go through it and we think that one day we're just going to come out the other side and everything's just going to be magical, we're going to have this big pile of money in our lap. We forget that each transaction that occurs in our business is actually where profit occurs, because we have to be thinking all the time about profit first. Once it's going to have it, it's not a random event that just goes on. The correct way to think, and I'm going to explain this logic to you so you really grasp it, is that sales minus profit equals expenses. Now, you probably hear that, and if you don't agree with it right away, allow me to explain it, but you agree with it, I'm excited. Hopefully you read profit first. That being said, let's go through this. This is called a small plate logic. Think about when you go out of your way and you're in a diet, if you've ever gone on a diet, what are some of the tricks and techniques that you use in order to lose weight? Most people forget the fundamental fact that psychology is one of the greatest ways that we can leverage ourselves in the results, period, point blank, zero. If you, for example, would go and have a big meal, and after that big meal, you get a little bit of a guilty remorse afterwards because you know that you had more than you should have, why don't you go out, throw all your plates away and get smaller plates? Because if you have a smaller plate and you load up that plate, guess what? You still have a smaller amount of food than what you would have on the large plate, but psychologically, your brain actually makes you full comparatively till when you have a larger plate, and you think psychologically that you should finish all the food that's on that plate, because a larger plate, even with a large amount of food, still doesn't look like a large amount of food. So it's all about the proportion size in our mind for what we think we can consume in relativity to the plate. Take that in. If I shrink my plate and I have less food on it, the plate looks like it still has a lot of food. My brain actually won't finish the amount of food that's on that plate because it looks like it's an abundance of food. It's the small plate logic. That's what you do when you follow this logic instead of this logic, because once you generate a sale, if you have a set percentages or a set amount of percentages that automatically distribute money or profits into different accounts for you, that's what you're left with for your expenses. Rather than your sales minus your expenses that are always justifiable whenever that big sale comes and you want to liquidate and expense everything that you have all of a sudden, if you're left with no profit again and you struggle until you find your next sale, stop that cycle. Just quit. Inside of this logic right here, you're always going to be broke and your business is always going to be broken because you're going to go out and generate sales in a crisis mindset because you're always going to be stressed financially and you're never going to be in a position where you ever get rid of the expenses that are always piling up and never going away. I'll tell you what, I ran my business like this for months until a month about 13 of my business where I was able to change everything. Just by understanding that whenever I generated a sale, I was going to immediately take a certain amount of money and put it into a profit account. I was immediately going to take a certain amount of money and put it into a tax hold account. I was immediately going to take a certain amount of money and put it into my operating expense account. And then anything that was left, I was going to use to pay bills or to pay whatever else it was that I needed to pay like payrolls or bonuses or anything like that. And you know what happened? Nothing. Nothing broken. Nothing changed. Nothing all of a sudden happened that ruins me. What actually happened is I started profiting in my business. I started making more money. I actually started taking over more myself. I started having less stress. I was able to do more things. And you know what? Nothing about it was unethical, bad or wrong. Everybody else in my business continued getting paid. I actually paid payroll with ease comparatively to some of the times when I felt like I was struggling. Because when it's in this logic, I'm not sitting aside to proper amounts. I'm going to my operating expense account in order to pay payroll and things like that. I'm making justified expenses because I have that lump sum in my account and I'm doing bank balance accounting. Most of you guys when you run your business or when you start your own business are going to run bank balance accounting. Meaning you're not going to be in a position where you actually have a take home profit. Because you're always going to look at your account and make emotional decisions on what you spend, when you spend it or what. And who you're paying for, you have that lump sum. Versus when you don't have that lump sum, you're going to scatter and scurry and you can go out and try to find that sale. It's going to say go and pay all of your bill all of a sudden and then you find it and you pay them off and then you're stuck in the same situation because you got more bills while you're in that scattering situation. See? Don't fuck yourself. Sales minus profit equals expenses, ladies and gentlemen. Generate sales, take out a flat percentage of whatever it is that's comfortable for you. I personally take 10% profit out of my business. Sometimes I take up to 30% depending on the size of the deal of the night. I site it off in different accounts. I have a profit account, I have a tax account, I have an operating expense account. The rest of it is justified as expenses that I pay off out of the operating expense account for whatever's left. If you guys want your business like this, you guys are going to have efficiency, ease, success, a stress free environment. You're always going to be paid. You're never going to have scarcity. You're always going to feel like you're getting something out of your business instead of you feeling like you're a slave to your business. Which some of you will get caught up in. You'll avoid the shell game, meaning you'll avoid having a large amount of expenses. They have to go out and get a big sale to cover while you're then generating expenses that you didn't have to cover with the next big sale, where you never get the actual profit that you think of as an event. Profit is a habit. It's not an event. It is a routine that you need to develop and it comes with this mindset. Figure out your percentages. If you need additional help understanding this logic, read a book called Profit First. If you don't need, I don't care. It's one of the best books you can possibly pick up in your entire life as a business owner and it requires three chapters or less to understand this entire concept. I look forward to seeing you guys in the next video. This was Profit First.
